reason: Both answers correctly identify the core rule: statutory damages require registration either before infringement began or within 3 months of first publication (§412), correctly compute that the mid-January grace window closed around mid-April, correctly state registration (not just application) is required to sue at all (Fourth Estate), and correctly note actual damages/injunction/DMCA remain available regardless. Both also flag the same nuance about whether ongoing infringement counts as one continuing act or resets with new infringers/timing — A hedges this as a circuit split (accurate framing), B states it more confidently as one continuing act while also carving out a 'new infringer' exception (also reasonably defensible, and arguably more actionable since it tells the user exactly what to go check — archive.org dates, etc.). B adds concrete, decision-relevant specifics (statutory damage ranges, filing costs, expedited registration option and cost, §504(b) citation) that make it more immediately actionable without straying into padding. A is tighter and slightly more cautious in its legal hedge, but B's added detail is genuinely useful rather than filler, and its structure (verdict up front, clear timeline, concrete next steps) makes it marginally more useful for a busy reader making a decision.
